concepts.title: Cash Flow Statement is a statement of changes in financial position
concepts.description: Cash Flow Statement (CFS) shows the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents of an enterprise during a given period. It provides a snapshot of the enterprise's liquidity and its ability to meet its short-term obligations.

concepts.title: Objective of Cash Flow Statement
concepts.description: The objective of CFS is to provide users of financial statements with a comprehensive picture of an enterprise's inflows, outflows, and net changes in cash and cash equivalents, which can help them in assessing the liquidity and financial flexibility of the enterprise.

concepts.title: Main Sections of Cash Flow Statement
concepts.description: The main sections of CFS are: (a) Cash Flows from Operating Activities (a) Cash Flows from Investing Activities (c) Cash Flows from Financing Activities (d) Net Increase / Decrease in Cash and Cash Equivalents.

concepts.title: CASH
concepts.description: CASH stands for: C - Cash Flows from Operating Activities A - Cash Flows from Investing Activities S - Cash Flows from Financing Activities H - Net Increase / Decrease in Cash and Cash Equivalents

concepts.title: Ignoring Non-Cash Items
concepts.description: Students often ignore non-cash items such as depreciation, amortization, and impairment losses when preparing CFS. However, these items are crucial in assessing the enterprise's liquidity and financial flexibility.

concepts.title: Cash and Cash Equivalents
concepts.description: Cash and cash equivalents include: (a) Cash in hand (b) Demand deposits (c) Short-term investments (d) Commercial paper

concepts.title: Cash Flow Statement is not a substitute for Balance Sheet and Income Statement
concepts.description: CFS provides a snapshot of an enterprise's liquidity and financial flexibility, but it is not a substitute for Balance Sheet and Income Statement, which provide a more comprehensive picture of an enterprise's financial position and performance.

concepts.title: Direct Method and Indirect Method
concepts.description: There are two methods of preparing CFS: (a) Direct Method (b) Indirect Method. Direct Method shows cash inflows and outflows, while Indirect Method shows net change in cash and cash equivalents.
